text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic dissection of lymph nodes in the left pariotic region, left retro-aortic region, root of the inferior mesenteric artery, and aorto-caval region in a fifty-year-old female patient with Knott-Cracker syndrome and rectal cancer.
The surgeon tents the inferior mesenteric artery, revealing a lymph node at its root. The dissection proceeds to separate the IMA root from the aorta, followed by transection of the IMA.

The surgeon carefully dissects the para-aortic lymph nodes, which are densely attached to the aortic wall. An engorged vein is visible on the lateral side of the para-aortic lymph node, extending from the pelvis to the level of the kidney, with a branch leading to the left kidney. The dissection involves separating the lymph nodes from the aorta, left ureter, and the engorged venous structure.
The para-aortic lymph node is removed en bloc, revealing another engorged venous structure located behind the aorta.
The surgeon removes the rectal cancer and performs intracorporeal anastomosis. The specimen reveals a rectal cancer along with a bulky para-aortic lymph node.

After the surgery, we reviewed the preoperative CT scan and noted there is a markedly stenosis of left renal vein to IVC, which resulted in engorged left renal vein, left ovarian vein, and left lumbar ascending vein. So, the first engorged venous structure is left ovarian vein. The other venous structure behind the aorta is left lumbar ascending vein.

On the horizontal view of the CT scan, we noted the compression of left renal vein between the SMA and aorta. The aortal mesenteric angle is twenty degree, which was compatible with the findings of the Nutcracker syndrome. We show it in this figure. Paralytic lymph node dissection should be very careful in this kind of patient.
